data.title: BHP Group: Making A Long-Term Bet On The Developing World
data.description:
Investment thesis.

BHP Group (BHP) is well-positioned to benefit from global industrialisation and infrastructure expansion. It is producing a broad range of crucial commodities that will be needed in growing volumes around the world, but especially in the developing world, where most of the world's economic expansion is taking place. I decided to make an initial investment in this stock, as its share price has declined by about 8% so far this month. It is currently trading at about half its value compared to its all-time high, with the intention of continuing to buy incrementally if it continues to decline from here.

FY 2025 results.

For the full fiscal year of 2025, BHP saw an 8% decline in revenues to $51.3 billion, while underlying attributable profits from operations declined to $10.2 billion, from $13.7 billion in FY 2024. It is a decent performance, given the less-than-favourable commodity price movements.

While copper prices worked in BHP's favour in the past year, iron ore and coal prices did not. Commodities markets have not been particularly kind to certain miners this year, especially those involved in extracting industrial minerals. Copper was the one major exception, and it highlights BHP's advantage as a diversified commodities producer, since its results would have been much worse this year without its copper segment.

The challenging market environment for miners, particularly for BHP, is leading to some degradation in its financial health.

While its net debt-to-market cap ratio is not particularly high, with a market cap currently at $140 billion versus $12.9 billion in net debt, the sizable increase in net debt compared with the previous year is a sign of a company and, arguably, an industry that needs better market terms for its long-term financial health. It should be noted that not all growth in the company's net debt can be attributed to its financial performance. The acquisition of a 50% interest in the Vicuna project cost $2.1 billion for the fiscal year of 2025. However, one would assume that if its financial performance were better, more of the acquisition cost would have been supported from internal resources.

Trading at a premium on a more diverse product lineup.

BHP is arguably trading at a premium compared with some of its mining peers; however, it can be argued that the premium is justified by its more diverse portfolio.

As we can see, after a robust recovery from the pandemic, BHP's share price has gradually moved on a downward trajectory over the past few years. The downward trajectory is by no means unjustified. Compared with mining peer Vale SA (VALE), for instance, which has a forward P/E of under 6, BHP's P/E ratio is more than double; therefore, this is clearly a reflection of its deteriorating financial performance, mostly driven by external market conditions that caused a decline in yearly profits since the decade highs reached in 2022.

At the same time, as I already pointed out, BHP is exhibiting resilience, thanks to its comparatively more diverse portfolio; therefore, one can argue that the higher valuation can be justified. VALE is tied to the iron ore market; thus, its results can be expected to be more volatile. BHP, on the other hand, can more steadily build on its current results in the event that external factors will work in its favor.

Betting on developing world demand.

When it comes to the commodities market, by far the biggest story of the past few decades has been the rise of China. With a highly industrialised and infrastructurally well-developed economy that is also slowing in terms of growth, China is no longer looking like the engine of commodities demand growth that it once was. This slowdown has been one of the main factors in keeping commodity prices in check in the past decade or so.

While China may be slowing significantly, and the economic growth we are seeing is becoming less commodities-intensive, several other Asian countries are taking up the mantle.

Unlike China, countries like India, Indonesia, or Vietnam still have a long way to go in terms of industrialising and building up their infrastructure.

The continent of Africa is also a potentially accelerating market for commodities. It is seeing GDP growth in the 4% range, and it is in great need of infrastructure. It is also industrialising at a decent pace, with the manufacturing of goods picking up pace, growing at a faster pace than the overall economy of the continent.

Just to get a sense of how much potential demand there may be in Africa, its population is increasing by about 2%/year, and as of now, about half the population lacks electricity in their homes, based on UN data. About a third lack access to water in their home. Household needs are just a glimpse. Africa will need transport infrastructure, irrigation, and a host of other things to meet growing needs.

We most often tend to fall into the trap of judging the world by what we see around us. Most citizens of the developed world see an abundance of infrastructure and industrial capacity; therefore, we may be tempted to conclude that there is little room left for demand growth. Looking at developing nations that are currently experiencing a robust rate of economic growth, the potential demand for industrial minerals is great, as long as economic growth can be maintained near current levels.

We may even see some regions, such as Europe, experience a pickup in commodities demand, as talk of reindustrialisation is starting to take hold. The Draghi Report, for instance, calls for Europe to start spending 800 billion/year on infrastructure, reindustrialising and innovating the European economy. I do not foresee this happening in the next year or so, but beyond that, perhaps once the Ukraine war ends and Europeans re-focus on economic issues, we could potentially see an industrial revival on the old continent.

BHP is well-positioned for global industrial revival.

If my thesis is correct, the effect of many countries that are currently seeing robust economic growth rates but are lacking in infrastructure and industrial development will be equivalent to a China 2.0 effect on global commodities.

As we can see, all major minerals that BHP produces are set to grow in volume. Of the four commodities represented in the chart above, copper arguably has the most solid market outlook.

I personally think that long-term demand for iron is also likely to be more robust than the market expects; however, there is far more uncertainty. For instance, we could see an infrastructure building boom in Africa, assuming it is not plagued by the constant instability characteristic of the region. Because copper is involved in the EV trend, the AI trend, and so on, the future of that market is not heavily dependent on whether places like Africa or Indonesia will undertake a rapid industrialisation and build out infrastructure. Iron is more directly and specifically connected to the industrialisation and development of underdeveloped nations, while copper is not.

Conclusions.

I am considering BHP as a long-term investment opportunity, with this being a good starting point for incremental buying. I am planning to add to my current position if its share price declines by a further 10% from here, as long as the decline is not related to internal, company-specific factors. The positive external factors would also have to remain in place.

It is hard to get a sense of whether its current valuation, with a forward P/E ratio of over 13, makes it a good buy. There is the possibility that BHP's output growth, combined with favourable market prices for its copper, iron, coal, and potash, will provide a significant long-term boost to its revenues and profits, and at the same time, its share price will not rise accordingly but rather result in the stock trading at a potentially lower P/E. This is the reason why I am proceeding cautiously in building my position in this stock by buying incrementally.

In the short term, many factors can move this stock up or down. We have the US tariffs situation as well as many economic & geopolitical frictions that can move the broader markets and BHP's stock price with it. We could see a sudden relaxation of these points of friction, which could move markets higher, or we could see a worsening, which could lead to a pullback. These factors will dictate whether I am adding to my position or not. The factor that has me interested in building a position in this stock is the long-term fundamentals picture I presented, which I find compelling. It may require some patience to see it pan out. BHP's 4% dividend helps to wait it out until we see those fundamentals kick in, which, combined with its growth profile, has the potential to drive this stock higher in the longer term.

Risks to my thesis.

BHP's low debt levels and its diverse product lineup that reduces the risk of any particular trend in global economic development catching it offside are all solid company-specific characteristics that make this a less risky bet. The main risk is that external trends will not work in the company's favor as I currently expect them to. I am particularly concerned about the seeming deterioration in the post-WWII world order. While it can bring opportunities as new economic powers emerge, it can also bring instability due to rising frictions. It is something to watch, in case major global economic disruptions occur as a result. Mining companies are particularly vulnerable due to their highly cyclical nature.

data.title: Snowflake: Powerful AI Story, But The Valuation Looks One Step Ahead
data.description:
Snowflake (SNOW) is a data storage company that experienced an AI-powered surge in stock price of 37% over the past year and outperformed the benchmark. The technology company is tied to elevated investor confidence, represented by a significant valuation - Snowflake is trading at a forward P/E of 197.

The cloud-based technology company has been a significant top- and bottom-line growth story so far. Over the past 12 months, SNOW delivered a double-digit business expansion, arguing for a premium valuation. However, based on Wall Street analysts, although the company delivers approximately 30% bottom-line growth annually through FY2031, it would still be trading at roughly 2x overvaluation versus the benchmark.

This indicates that the market may have run ahead of the company's fundamentals, which leaves little room for error. Snowflake remains a phenomenal company, but it is difficult to justify the AI disruptor trading at such valuations. Over the next five years, there appears to be a lot of uncertainty, and the current valuation seems to be overly extended.

I think Snowflake may consolidate in the near term. Although I don't expect a significant sell-off, I believe alternative costs may be too high to hold onto the name. I rate Snowflake a Sell with a $234 price target over the next year. I anticipate the stock to consolidate, trading in a range, but remaining flat over the next year. This would indicate broader market underperformance and high opportunity costs.

FQ2 2026: The Significant Growth Story Remains Intact

On August 27, the data storage company delivered FQ2 2026 results. This was a substantial quarter for the company, highlighting an impressive, nearly triple-digit bottom-line surge and rapid top-line expansion. The market reacted with a 20% surge over the following trading day.

Snowflake posted $1.14 billion in revenue, highlighting a 32% growth on a year-over-year basis and $57 million above what the market had anticipated. The bottom line arrived at $0.35 in diluted EPS, roughly doubled over the past year versus $0.18 in FQ2 2025, and $0.08 above analysts' expectations. This justifies premium valuation and argues for a positive outlook.

In the earnings presentation, Snowflake highlighted that the company is experiencing solid momentum in demand. More importantly, this led to a sticky customer base, justified by a 125% in net revenue retention rate. The company exceeded 12,000 total customers, with 654 of them with more than $1 million in spending. This number has increased from 504 in FQ2 2025, indicating a 30% surge on a year-over-year basis.

This highlights a strong market position and high-quality business, which fuels a positive outlook. In addition, out of the Forbes Global 2000, 751 names are SNOW's customers. I think this appears to be impressive and indicates a significant presence in the high-growth landscape.

Snowflake highlighted significant customer commitments. Management underscored $6.9 billion in RPO, with roughly 50% of the estimate to be converted into top-line contribution within the following 12 months. This significantly contributes to the company's positive outlook. Wall Street estimates $4.61 billion in FY2026 revenue, and if half of RPO is recognized, this will account for 75% of the market's expectations.

Indicating that the technology company achieved 19% growth in total customers on a year-over-year basis from 10,147 in FQ2 2025 to 12,062 in FQ2 2026, if the trend continues, the top-line estimate may come in conservative. This suggests Snowflake remains a phenomenal company with positive tailwinds, which may be driving business expansion over the following quarters.

Management underlined that it has achieved 76% in non-GAAP product gross margin, which has remained flat on a year-over-year basis, reflecting business stability. The non-GAAP operating margin surged from 5% to 11% over the past 12 months, indicating significant improvement in profitability. This supports continued bottom-line expansion.

On the downside, the company remains heavily dependent on revenue derived from the Americas. This accounts for 78% of total revenue, and although this has slightly decreased from 79% in the same quarter last year, the concentration remains. This may cloud the outlook if sentiment and IT spending shift.

I believe Snowflake remains a phenomenal business, highlighting a solid demand and continued customer growth. The company operates at premium margins and offers high-quality services, justified by a sticky customer base. This should support premium valuation in the following quarters; yet, highlighting a significant triple-digit earnings multiple, it may be challenging to justify continued multiple expansion. I think it is likely to consolidate, unless management unlocks a new revenue growth cycle, resulting in margin expansion and significant revenue growth.

What to Expect From FQ3 2026?

Snowflake is anticipated to deliver FQ3 2026 on December 3, after the market closes. Wall Street is expecting $1.18 billion in revenue, up 26% on a year-over-year basis, and $0.31 in diluted EPS, representing a 56% surge over the past 12 months. I believe the company is well-positioned to achieve these results, justified by substantial RPO, solid momentum in customer growth, and AI-related spending.

In the earnings presentation, management guided for FY2026. Overall, SNOW expects a continuation of strong performance. Anticipation is also roughly in line with my and the market's expectations.

Nevertheless, I think it may be slightly concerning that Snowflake anticipates a decline in non-GAAP product gross margin. Although it would remain at elevated levels, this could suggest the metric has topped. This could partially cloud the future outlook and suppress sky-high investor sentiment, validated by nearly 200x earnings multiple.

On the upside, Snowflake expects an improvement in non-GAAP operating margin, which supports my thesis that the bottom line is likely to continue expanding further.

It will be interesting to monitor whether SNOW delivers another outstanding quarterly report next week. This would reflect the AI disruptor dominating the space further, arguing for a bullish narrative. Nevertheless, based on the significant valuation, the market knows SNOW is a phenomenal business, and I argue that its future revenue growth may already be reflected in the stock price. This could limit upside in the short term and support my consolidation thesis.

Triple-Digit Earnings Multiple Argues for a Breather

Snowflake currently trades at a forward P/E of approximately 197. This represents about 9x overvaluation versus the sector median and the average company in the S&P 500 trading at roughly 22x earnings multiple. Although this highlights a massive premium, the chart above indicates this has been a typical business valuation over the past few years.

However, I argue that the market knows SNOW is a phenomenal company and that its future revenue growth may already be priced in the stock price. This is supported by the forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 16.79 and 17.20, respectively, implying a 5x premium at the midpoint versus sector medians of 3.16 and 3.13. This also suggests that even if management continues delivering such an impressive revenue growth further, it may not necessarily translate into stock price appreciation.

On the upside, Snowflake remains a low-leverage business. The AI name has $2.69 billion in total obligations, which, although it may appear significant, it has $3.59 billion in cash; thus, it could pay the debt with ease. This is a solid argument for a positive outlook, indicating that management has room to use debt-related instruments if needed.

The technology company has been an outstanding top-line growth story so far. However, its performance may not justify such an inflated earnings multiple. Over the past 12 months, SNOW achieved 28% revenue growth on a year-over-year basis, implying a 2.8x outperformance versus the average company in the S&P 500 at 10% historical revenue growth. This also represents a 3.3x outperformance versus peers at 8.44%.

The forward diluted EPS growth rate of 19% suggests a 53% advantage versus peers at 12% and 90% outperformance versus the benchmark at 10% bottom-line growth over the past few years. Although this indicates significant market position, strength in demand, and an advantage over peers, I don't think it justifies a 9x premium valuation. Snowflake deserves a higher multiple versus peers and the average company in the S&P 500, but such a difference creates a massive disconnect in valuation, which I believe may narrow over the following quarters.

SNOW operates at an above-market gross profit margin (TTM) of 67%, representing a 34% advantage versus peers at 50%. The technology company generated approximately $838 million in cash from operations (TTM), reflecting a 6x outperformance versus peers at $123 million. Although this highlights premium performance, I don't think this justifies such a premium earnings multiple.

To conclude, Snowflake remains a phenomenal business that may continue to dominate the space further, yet I feel that the market may have run ahead of the company's fundamentals. The AI disruptor grows top and bottom lines at a higher pace than peers, but I am concerned the performance does not justify such a premium multiple. It suggests that management has little room for error, requires excellent execution over the next few years, and the downside doesn't appear to be protected if sentiment shifts. I think opportunity costs seem to be too high to hold the name.

Wall Street forecasts $1.19 in diluted EPS for FY2026 and $1.64 in diluted EPS for FY2027. I think the company remains well-positioned to achieve these targets, followed by strong performance, momentum in demand, AI-driven tailwinds, and significant RPO backlog. However, this would put the company at a 143x earnings multiple, which would suggest about a 7x premium to the benchmark. I think unless SNOW unlocks a new revenue growth catalyst, it is likely to consolidate, as it could be challenging for the market to justify expansion from these inflated levels.

Therefore, if we apply a 143x earnings multiple, about a 25% contraction from current levels, to the FY2027 EPS estimate of $1.64, we arrive at my price target of $234. I think the company remains well-positioned to improve its fundamentals, but I also believe the market may have already priced in future revenue growth, capping the stock price performance. It will be interesting to see whether investors are willing to pay a higher premium for the stock, but I think opportunity costs may appear to be elevated, and earnings multiple expansion seems limited.

Main Risks and Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Snowflake that could meaningfully impact stock price performance in the short term.

To begin with, the company trades at a significant premium. A nearly 200x forward P/E indicates significant optimism and elevated investor confidence. Although SNOW remains a high-quality business, based on my analysis, it is difficult to justify the technology name trading at such a premium. If sentiment in the market changes, Snowflake is likely to correct from these levels.

The company is heavily dependent on AI-powered demand. Therefore, if IT spending slows down or heavy CapEx investments into AI don't translate into meaningful top- and bottom-line growth, sentiment around the AI theme could dramatically shift, and this would cloud the company's outlook. In addition to this, a substantial amount of revenue derives from the Americas, which highlights dependency on these markets.

Management indicated expectations of roughly 50% of RPO to be converted into top-line contribution over the next 12 months. This accounts for about 75% of Wall Street estimates for FY2026. This comes with execution and conversion risks. If the company can't convert the anticipated RPO amount, the top-line growth could decelerate, and it would be even more challenging to justify a premium multiple.

What Could Go Positive for the Stock?

There are also reasons why the technology company is trading at such a phenomenal premium. It has been a steady performer over the past years, and the market knows SNOW is likely to deliver.

Snowflake experienced double-digit growth in customer numbers, representing solid momentum in demand. The company also achieved a significant increase in clients' spending above $1 million, and roughly 30% of Forbes Global 2000 are choosing Snowflake as the service provider. This implies a substantial moat and strong market position. If the trend continues, the market may be willing to pay a premium further.

The company remains low-leveraged and is well-positioned to operate in both high- and low-interest rate environments. This is also arguing for a premium valuation.

Nevertheless, I think that Snowflake remains a significant company; I am not sure whether it appears to be compelling at these inflated levels. I argue it may consolidate for a while, and the earnings multiple could contract. This suggests opportunity costs may appear too high to consider the name.

Conclusion

Snowflake is a dominant name in the data storage landscape and experiences significant AI-powered tailwinds, which may fuel a positive narrative further. It continues rapid top- and bottom-line growth, remains a low-leveraged business, and operates at premium margins. The trend is likely to continue over the following quarters.

However, based on the analysis and triple-digit valuation, it seems that the market has pulled valuation forward by multiple years. Even if the company delivers robust performance for the next five years and achieves analysts' targets, by FY2031, it would still be trading at a 2x premium versus the benchmark. This indicates overextended optimism, and it appears to be challenging to justify the company's significant valuation.

Therefore, I argue Snowflake is a Sell with a $234 price target over the next 12 months. I believe the company may trade in a range and consolidate, which could result in flat performance over the next year. 51 Wall Street analysts present a $272 price target over the next 12 months, suggesting a 17% upside possibility. Although this is definitely a potential, I don't feel bullish about the stock at the current valuations and believe opportunity costs are too high at this price tag. It will be interesting to monitor if my expectations materialize.

data.title: 2026 Investor Guide To Western LiDAR Companies
data.description:
Background

As we enter 2026, the most important thing for LiDAR investors is to observe a broader shift toward physical AI and its expanding role across non-automotive sectors. Adoption is still in the early stages, but investors will increasingly need to distinguish between companies positioned for long-term commercial relevance and those still operating as early-stage startups. My goal is to identify which Western LiDAR companies appear capable of benefiting from this transition as the industry consolidates again.

In many ways, it feels as if little progress has been made in LiDAR adoption in the West. Nonetheless, adoption is happening across sectors not traditionally associated with LiDAR, and this is where the long-term opportunity now appears strongest.

The term "physical AI" describes AI driving a technological shift in the physical world, comparable to the first industrial revolution. A replacement of humans with robots and automation.

In the West, applications of LiDAR sensors in security, intelligent transportation systems, robotics, and robotaxis surpassed revenues in what was once considered the primary use for LiDAR—ADAS applications at various automation levels. Companies focused exclusively on ADAS struggle, while those targeting broader applications, including Ouster (OUST), perform better and are expected to continue doing so as LiDAR adoption expands into physical-AI markets rather than passenger vehicles.

In contrast, the sole focus on automotive is likely to claim its first casualty: a former flagship of automotive LiDAR applications, Luminar (LAZR).

AI-themed companies became a rollercoaster of volatility with valuations based on the role physical AI plays in revenues and operational focus. The market recently sold off amid concerns about an AI bubble, and stock prices have fallen to lower levels, creating undervalued conditions.

In my review, I will not evaluate deals, customer brand names, or technology, thereby avoiding a focus on narrative. Instead, I analyze each company's revenue and gross margins, with an emphasis on the price-to-sales ratio to identify startup characteristics. I assess the available cash to extend the runway and regard operating cash flow (OCF) consumption as a primary indicator, believing that positive OCF will eventually lead to profitability, which is a crucial indicator for investors.

LiDAR companies to avoid in 2026

I'm going to start the 2026 review with the worst conditions and situations investors should avoid.

Luminar's future is bleak. While there's speculation about refinancing and new funding, the company cannot cover its operational costs or pay interest on its debt. It lost its largest customer, Volvo. Although market speculation is driven by talks of potential opportunities to save the company, in my opinion, its public lifespan is challenging.

The second worst in the group is MicroVision (MVIS). MicroVision achieved very little in sales during the period I wrote about LiDAR. It failed to secure contracts for its products and is now attempting to reinvent itself by repeating the same strategy: acquiring another insolvent company. According to CEO Glenn DeVos, the current product lineup is being reevaluated into a tri-sensor concept, with samples expected by the end of 2026. As a result, revenue forecasts are a fraction of what they were just a month ago.

During Q3, the company reported $99M in cash and $47M available through the ATM offering, leaving it well-funded through 2026 despite paying $32M of debt in quarterly installments.

The primary concern I see is that the company is falling behind in technology, as demonstrated by its acquisitions of companies with completely different technological platforms. Since the company purchased failed startups out of bankruptcy, there is a high risk that they will not develop into viable commercial products. The original sensor, MAVIN, has remained unchanged for the past two years and has not gained any customers. Despite MicroVision's claim of years of LiDAR experience, I would classify it as a startup with unproven technology, not an investment opportunity.

2026 Forecasts: An Unbiased Take

The table below reflects the forecast for the next three years. The quarterly OCF is based on the last reported quarter, Q3 2025. The spend is calculated by multiplying quarterly OCF by 4 for each year.

The forecasts consist of analysts' estimates averaged by SA. The first warning sign in the table is the Luminar estimates. The company probably won't exist in its current form, if at all, in 2026. The 2028 estimate of $445M is clearly unrealistic for a company without series production and facing severe challenges.

Innoviz's (INVZ) estimates are also uncertain for 2026. In its latest Q3 report, the company reported year-to-date revenue of $29.8M from NREs and $7M from machinery sold to a customer. Sensor sales totaled only $4.3M in 2025, a slight decrease of $20K compared to 2024. The $83M forecast for 2026 primarily consists of NREs, with some anticipated increase in sensor revenue; however, this projection may be overstated. While Innoviz claims to have doubled its revenue in 2025, most of that growth is non-recurring engineering expense, or NRE, that cannot be counted on to repeat in the future.

In the 10Q filing for Q3, Innoviz shows future NREs only as $12.8M without reflecting them in the balance sheet, and $2.8M in deferred revenue, totaling $15.6M. The company narrative targets $30M–$60M of NREs to be included in the 2026 estimate; however, this should be approached with caution, as there is no guarantee it will happen. To complicate things, Innoviz has not yet begun commercialization efforts, which will be costly and increase operating cash flow (OCF) spending that seems under control today. With all those red flags, Innoviz remains in a startup phase and will continue to behave as one in 2026.

During the first three quarters of 2025, Aeva (AEVA) generated twice the sensor revenue of Innoviz, at $8.3M, but recorded only $4M in NREs, suggesting that the OEM deal structures differ. Innoviz’s customers cover most development costs, while Aeva relies on high operating cash flow consumption to fund its own development. Aeva averages over $30M per quarter in spending, ranking second only to Luminar. Minimal revenue growth classifies the company as a startup, and its projections for the next three years do not change that view—especially disappointing in 2027, when full recognition of its Daimler Truck collaboration was expected. Consequently, high OCF spending will require additional funding.

While Innoviz continues to draw from its ATM, Aeva's financial outlook is not as bleak as the table suggests. The company still has $35M in pending equity sales with LG, has received $100M from Apollo for a convertible note to be recognized in Q4, and has a $123M preferred stock facility from its largest investor, Sylebra.

Yet, Aeva’s high OCF consumption creates significant cash-shortage risk. Even with funding from Apollo and the conversion of warrants into shares, Aeva’s spending will leave it with only $34M in cash by the end of 2026, likely forcing it to tap the Sylebra facility. While the company has funding sources, these will inevitably lead to stock dilution and impact its valuation.

In contrast, Ouster is best positioned among its peers, and it's expected to reduce its cash on hand by only $11M in 2026 and become OCF-positive in 2027. Based on my table, profitability is projected for 2028. I do not anticipate market-driven dilution; going forward, any dilution should come solely from stock-based compensation.

Valuation

The data below reflects the share price from Nov 21. Using my expected 10x ratio, both Aeva and MicroVision are overvalued, while the rest are undervalued.

Luminar's condition is a result of its near-reorganization pricing, so it should not be considered an opportunity. I leave it up to investors to weigh the cautionary tale about Innoviz's revenue if it does indeed represent an undervalued situation. Seeing a company sell fewer sensors in 2025 than in 2024 is discouraging. While NRE volume is forecasted to increase, these efforts should lead to real revenue, but that remains to be seen.

That leaves Ouster, which I see as undervalued. The company's market valuation is not for the faint of heart. The price fluctuation has caused Ouster to reach $41.65 as a 52-week high and drop to the $6s seen just in April of this year.

The current assumed AI bubble sell-off has affected all companies, and Ouster now sits at 6 P/S. I think if the company can continue growing at 30 to 50% in revenue and maintain a mid-range gross margin of 38.5%, it makes one of the most solid investment options out there, not just in LiDAR but also in the AI thematic.

I recently wrote an article on Ouster, and I believe the estimates I provided still stand. The company has multiple projects in progress, and the lack of new product announcements may be a strategic decision aimed at meeting existing contracts and reducing inventory. The only adjustment is that doubling the TAM can wait until 2026.

Moreover, when I read the Q3 transcript, I see the DF (digital flash sensor) timeline-to-market is likely to separate from OS REV8 in 2026, with the CEO suggesting that automotive stars will align in the next two years, meaning 2027 or perhaps 2028.

I cannot deny, though, that Ouster's decision tree, which led to the positive conclusion not to pursue consumer automotive, has contributed to its overall success today. As my analysis has shown, there has been little revenue from automotive for others, with no large-scale series production left, making that pursuit financially unattractive. In fact, other companies, in survival mode, are beginning to announce attempts to expand into different verticals, pursuing what Ouster does.

Conclusion

For 2026, I see Ouster as an attractive investment opportunity, with current pricing indicating an undervaluation. For price targets, I recommend my recent article on Ouster.

Based on the OCF of just $24.5M over the three quarters, the company has about 90 months of cash, or about 7.5 years, a stat that will continue to improve as revenue and cash flow increase.

Aside from Ouster, I see Innoviz, which has secured NREs, in a more advanced position than Aeva since NREs provide validation for its projects. I can understand an argument that Aeva's market cap is twice that of Innoviz's, suggesting Innoviz's share price could double. I continue to recommend Innoviz as a buy.

Based on the P/S ratio, Aeva appears overvalued. Currently, I consider it a hold for Aeva and do not recommend the stock.

2026 offers hope for expanded LiDAR applications and increased adoption of LiDAR in physical AI. As the LiDAR industry consolidates again, the remaining players are likely to benefit, though not equally. To clarify who will benefit the most, I continue to believe that revenue, gross margin, and operating cash flow are the best indicators for investing in LiDAR companies.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Voya Global Advantage And Premium Opportunity Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

  • During the quarter, the Fund underperformed its Index due to lower beta positioning. Conversely, the core model, particularly the sentiment pillar, and higher dividend yield contributed the most.
  • Inflation is easing, labor markets are softening, and the U.S. Federal Reserve has pivoted to rate cuts. Despite tariff pressures, consumer sensitivity and constrained spending should limit inflation persistence. U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) growth is projected to remain positive but moderate, with large-cap equities favored due to strong fundamental factors and artificial intelligence (AI)-driven growth potential.
  • Global momentum is mixed. Europe and the United Kingdom face fiscal and structural headwinds, while Japan shows promise amid reforms. China struggles with property sector stress and cautious consumers, though offshore equities benefit from technology exposure. International bonds offer less attractive yields, and emerging markets (EM) debt faces uneven fundamental factors.

Portfolio review

For the quarter, the Voya Global Advantage and Premium Opportunity Fund (IGA) provided a total return of 4.40% on a net asset value basis and a total return of 2.51% on a market price basis. For the same period, the Fund's reference Index, the MSCI World Value Index, returned 5.83%.

During the quarter, the Fund's equity sleeve underperformed the Index. The largest detractor among factor performance was the lower beta positioning, while the core model, particularly the sentiment pillar, and higher dividend yield contributed.

On the regional level, portfolio holdings in the United States and Japan detracted, and holdings in the Asia Ex-Japan and Europe–Non-Euro contributed the most.

On the sector level, stock selection in the financials, information technology, and materials sectors detracted. Among the key detractors were not owning Oracle Corp. (ORCL), owning a non-benchmark in Thomson Reuters Corp. (TRI), and not owning Intel Corp. (INTC)

By contrast, stock selection in the consumer staples, communication services, and utilities sectors contributed the most. Among the key individual stock contributors were not owning Salesforce Inc. (CRM), an underweight position in Meta Platforms Inc. Class A (META), and an overweight position in Central Japan Railway Co. (OTCPK:CJPRY)

Option portfolio

During the quarter, the Fund's option strategy had a positive impact on returns. The strategy seeks to generate premiums and retain some potential for upside appreciation. The Fund implements this strategy by typically writing call options on regional indices, the selection and allocation of which resulted from an optimization intended to track the reference Index of the Fund closely. The strike prices of the options written were typically out of the money or near the money with expiration dates around six weeks at inception.

Current strategy and outlook

Global value stocks performed well during the quarter.

U.S. and European value stocks led, while Japanese value stocks lagged. Sector winners included financials and energy, which benefited from stable yields and stronger commodity prices. Information technology struggled, as many value-oriented tech names could not keep pace with growth-oriented stocks.

Looking ahead, value stocks could keep doing well if interest rates remain stable and global demand for energy and industrial goods persists. However, to maintain that momentum, companies in these sectors will need to continue delivering steady profits and proving that they can handle slower growth conditions.

Holdings detail

Companies mentioned in this report—percentage of Fund investments, as of 09/30/25: Oracle Corp. 0.00%, Thomson Reuters Corp. 0.27%, Intel Corp. 0.00%, Salesforce Inc. 0.00%, Meta Platforms Inc. 2.20% and Central Japan Railway Co. 0.60%; 0% indicates that the security is no longer in the portfolio. Portfolio holdings are subject to change daily.

Voya Global Advantage and Premium Opportunity Fund (IGA)

Total Return and Income From Global Value Investments

Susanna JacobPortfolio Manager

Justin Montminy, CFAPortfolio Manager

Strategy overview

The Fund combines an actively-managed quantitative equity investment strategy with a call writing option strategy, seeking to create a diversified portfolio with enhanced total return potential and strong downside capture over a full market cycle.

An investor should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund(s) carefully before investing. For a free copy of the Fund's prospectus or summary prospectus, which contains this and other information, visit us at www.voyainvestments.com or call (800) 992-0180. Please read all materials carefully before investing.

The MSCI World Value Net Index captures large and mid-capitalization securities exhibiting overall value style characteristics across developed-market countries around the world. The value investment style characteristics for index construction are defined using three variables: book value to price, 12-month forward earnings to price, and dividend yield. The "Net" version of this index assumes the deduction of a withholding tax on dividend income applicable to non-resident, institutional investors who do not benefit from double taxation treaties. Returns presented for the benchmark index assumes the reinvestment of dividends after the deduction of withholding taxes, using (for international indices) a tax rate applicable to non-resident institutional investors who do not benefit from double taxation treaties. Index returns do not reflect fees, brokerage commissions, taxes or other expenses of investing. Investors cannot invest directly in an index.

All investing involves risks of fluctuating prices and the uncertainties of rates of return and yield inherent in investing. You could lose money on your investment and any of the following risks, among others, could affect investment performance. The following principal risks are presented in alphabetical order which does not imply order of importance or likelihood: Company; Currency; Derivative Instruments; Dividend; Environmental, Social, and Governance (Quantitative); Foreign (Non-U.S.) Investments/ Developing and Emerging Markets; Investment Model; Liquidity; Manager; Market; Market Capitalization; Market Disruption and Geopolitical; Operational; Option Writing; Other Investment Companies; Securities Lending. Investors should consult the Fund's Prospectus and Statement of Additional Information for a more detailed discussion of the Fund's risks. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing. Consider the Fund's investment objective, risks, and charges and expenses carefully before investing.

The Fund may purchase put and call options and may write (Sell) put options and call options and is subject to Options Risk. The risk in writing a call option is that the Fund gives up the opportunity for profit if the market price of the security increases and the option is exercised. The risk in buying an option is that the Fund pays a premium whether or not the option is exercised. Risks may also arise from an illiquid secondary market or from the inability of counter-parties to meet the terms of the contract. When an option is exercised or closed out, the Fund may be required to sell portfolio securities or to deliver portfolio securities to satisfy its obligations when it would not otherwise choose to do so, or the Fund may choose to sell portfolio securities to realize gains to offset the losses realized upon option exercise. Such sales or delivery would involve transaction costs borne by the Fund and may also result in realization of taxable capital gains, including short-term capital gains taxed at ordinary income tax rates, and may adversely impact the Fund's after-tax returns.

The strategy employs a quantitative model to execute the strategy. Data imprecision, software or other technology malfunctions, programming inaccuracies and similar circumstances may impair the performance of these systems, which may negatively affect performance. Furthermore, there can be no assurance that the quantitative models used in managing the strategy will perform as anticipated or enable the strategy to achieve its objective.

The Fund discussed may be available to you as part of your employer sponsored retirement plan. There may be additional plan level fees resulting in personal performance to vary from stated performance. Please call your benefits office for more information.

This commentary has been prepared by Voya Investment Management for informational purposes. Nothing contained herein should be construed as (i) an offer to sell or solicitation of an offer to buy any security or (ii) a recommendation as to the advisability of investing in, purchasing or selling any security. data.title: Retail Sales Up 0.2% In September, Lower Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Census Bureau's Advance Retail Sales Report for September showed consumer spending was lower than expected, with headline sales rising 0.2% versus the expected 0.4% monthly growth. This is down from August's 0.6% growth and marks the fourth consecutive monthly increase in headline sales.

For an inflation-adjusted perspective on retail sales, take a look at our Real Retail Sales commentary.

Here is the introduction from Tuesday's report:

Advance Estimates of U.S. Retail and Food ServicesAdvance estimates of U.S. retail and food services sales for September 2025, adjusted for seasonal variation and holiday and trading-day differences, but not for price changes, were $733.3 billion, up 0.2 percent (±0.4 percent)* from the previous month, and up 4.3 percent (±0.5 percent) from September 2024. Total sales for the July 2025 through September 2025 period were up 4.5 percent (±0.4 percent) from the same period a year ago. The July 2025 to August 2025 percent change was unrevised from up 0.6 percent (±0.2 percent).Retail trade sales were up 0.1 percent (±0.5 percent)* from August 2025, and up 3.9 percent (±0.5 percent) from last year. Nonstore retailers were up 6.0 percent (±1.2 percent) from last year, while food service and drinking places were up 6.7 percent (±1.8 percent) from September 2024.

The chart below is a log-scale snapshot of retail sales since the early 1990s. The three exponential regressions through the data help us to evaluate the long-term trend of this key economic indicator.

  1. The light purple line is a linear regression through the complete data series.
  2. The green line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2007 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the financial crisis.
  3. The blue line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2019 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the COVID-19 pandemic.

Monthly retail sales have been above the light purple and blue line since March 2021, signaling increased consumer spending that was most likely pent up as a result of the pandemic.

The year-over-year percent change provides another perspective on the historical trend. Current retail sales are up 4.3% compared to one year ago. Here is the headline series with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Core Retail Sales

Core sales (ex Autos) were up 0.3% in September. This is down from August's 0.6% reading and was consistent with expectations.

Core retail sales are up 4.1% compared to one year ago. Here is the year-over-year chart of core retail sales with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Retail Sales: “Control” Purchases

The next two charts illustrate retail sales “control” purchases, which is an even more “core” view of retail sales. This series excludes motor vehicles & parts, gasoline, building materials, as well as food services & drinking places.

The popular financial press typically ignores this series, but it's a more consistent and reliable reading of the economy. Retail sales control purchases fell -0.1% in September. This is down from August's 0.6% reading and was lower than the expected 0.3% growth in control sales.

Similar to the retail sales snapshot chart earlier, the chart below is a log-scale snapshot of control purchases since the early 1990s and includes two of the exponential regressions previously mentioned.

Here is the same series year-over-year. Current control purchases are up 4.2% compared to one year ago.

For a better sense of the reduced volatility of the “control” series, here is a YoY overlay with the headline retail sales. Note that the two series follow each other closely, but headline sales have more extreme highs and lows than the control series.

Retail sales will impact interest in the SPDR S&P Retail ETF (XRT), VanEck Retail ETF (RTH), Amplify Online Retail ETF (IBUY), and ProShares Online Retail ETF (ONLN).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Snowflake: Don't Be Fooled By Non-GAAP Metrics
data.description:
This is the last image in the Snowflake (SNOW) presentation.

If, like me, you are a fundamental investor, you will certainly be tempted to invest in this growth story. It is basically everything an analyst wants to see in a company's operations. Customers are growing rapidly and are already at a level that gives them consolidated status, the net revenue retention rate is over 120% (showing strong customer loyalty), and revenue is growing 30% YoY, not to mention the very compelling industry in which Snowflake is.

The problem is that my enthusiasm for Snowflake only went so far. Looking at the financials, the company is not yet as mature as it appears, with GAAP margins still under pressure (even though non-GAAP margins are already healthy) due to significant shareholder dilution. Therefore, even with interesting potential for this market, my rating for SNOW is a sell.

There Are Positives in the SNOW Case

Before moving on to the more negative side of the SNOW case, it is worth highlighting some of the company's positives. As mentioned, the highlights are truly impressive, and some points, such as strong revenue growth and many large customers, already show that Snowflake has indeed managed to create a product that users like, remain loyal to the ecosystem, and even increase their contracts. For instance, the company already has 751 customers from the Forbes Global 2000 list.

Another very interesting point is its market, which is closely related to data, AI, and machine learning. According to Snowflake, we are talking about a total addressable market of $170 billion in 2024, with estimates to grow to $355 billion in 2029. If we believe that the company can capture just 10% of this market, we are talking about revenue of $35 billion, much higher than the current revenue of ~$4.6 billion and much higher than market estimates of $10 billion for fiscal year 2030.

For these 2 reasons, the prospects for Snowflake's financials are, in a way, positive. The market sees topline compounding at a rate of around ~25% per year in the coming years, which seems to me to be a very feasible model. With the gains in operating leverage, along with efficiency and potentially lower dilution, the consensus for EPS is already much better at around 40% per year growth.

Snowflake: Valuation is Not Good

Well, if EPS has the potential to grow 40% per year over the next 3 fiscal years, what is the problem with the valuation? It's that the market already prices this in. In fiscal year 2028, growing at this rate, the P/E would still be 101x, as the table below shows.

Even if we say that the company will continue to grow at this rate after that, it will still take years for the shareholder yield to be minimally attractive. In fiscal year 2031, EPS will not even be $6, and today SNOW stock is trading for over $240.

Some listed peers from related industries, such as MongoDB (MDB) and Cloudflare (NET), are also trading at very high multiples, even similar to Snowflake, and deliver similar growth, which makes Snowflake seem not so out of place at first glance.

But we must remember that companies such as Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), and Microsoft (MSFT) also manage to exploit products similar to Snowflake's and still pose a competitive risk. All these 3 companies are infinitely cheaper than Snowflake, although they also have much more diversified businesses and are less exposed to this growth.

SBC is Very Misleading for SNOW

Stock-based compensation (SBC) is crucial to understanding why the valuation is even worse than it seems. The P/E ratios of 100x+ are based on non-GAAP EPS, and when we look at GAAP, it's much worse.

I understand that cash flow is not affected by this expense, as we can see below, the net loss for the first 6 months of 2025 was -$727 million, but after adding mainly depreciation (~$100 million) and SBC (~$780 million), which are somewhat recurring, there is a positive cash flow from operating activities of over $300 million. Even though this is not much for a company with a market cap of $70 billion+, it is a way of showing that cash flow is healthy.

However, in my opinion, this is very misleading. Shareholders are being diluted, and it is not as if this SBC is just a non-cash expense that will occur only once. It is a way of compensating employees. So at first glance, the SNOW’s operating margin, which is 11% non-GAAP, seems to be doing well and improving (since in Q2 2024 it was 5%), but the GAAP operating margin is -30%, much better than the -41% delivered last year, but still a very negative margin. The company will have to scale this revenue significantly until the shareholder value is really interesting and compensates for all this dilution.

Risks for the Bear Case

The two main risks for Snowflake's case are dilution and valuation. But for the bear case, there are also some risks.

The main one is economies of scale. If the company continues to grow and surprise the market (which will require very strong execution), we may see revenue growing at a much faster pace than expenses such as SBC, and consequently GAAP net income would improve, as would valuation.

I don't necessarily hope that SNOW stock will perform poorly, especially since I don't have a short position. But I believe that a correction is quite possible in this scenario, and the risk that the correction will not happen is precisely that the market mood will remain very optimistic about the case, pricing SNOW as a quality compounder for the coming decades and consequently maintaining this premium valuation.

Q3 Earnings and the Bottom Line

Note that Snowflake's Q3 will be next week, and the market expects an EPS of $0.31. While a beat here is important and could spark optimism about the stock, I still prefer to look at other metrics.

Top-line growth will be extremely important because, for the future prospects of a better valuation to be possible, there needs to be continued compounding on the top line, which is precisely why management needs to continue to show that operations are running smoothly and prospects are good.

Finally, at the current level, I would like to start seeing greater concern with SBC and shareholder dilution, showing that the increase in revenue is helping to dilute costs and expenses, including SBC.

In short, I still think Snowflake is a good company; its operations are practically flawless, its prospects are very strong, and its financials are also quite good to a certain extent, but the valuation is not at all compatible with a company that will still take years and years to reach a reasonable non-GAAP P/E, let alone a cheap GAAP multiple (it could take more than a decade, even with compounding).

Therefore, I am initiating my coverage of SNOW with a sell rating.

data.title: Hartford Dynamic Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US fixed-income markets produced positive total returns in the third quarter of 2025, as political uncertainty and fiscal sustainability concerns prevailed. The US reached several major trade agreements—including with the EU, UK, and Japan—while its trade truce with China was extended for 90 days. US data integrity came under scrutiny after the dismissal of the Bureau of Labor Statistics commissioner and after the institutional credibility concerns surfaced following the attempted removal of Fed Governor Lisa Cook, ultimately blocked by the Supreme Court. Credit spreads 1 tightened further and most fixed-income sectors outperformed US Treasuries.

Performance Summary

  • Hartford Dynamic Bond Fund (I Share) generated positive total returns during the quarter, driven by lower yields and tighter spreads. Treasury positioning contributed to performance. Yields declined in the US as the Federal Reserve (Fed) delivered its first rate cut of 2025, indicating a shift in focus from inflation to labor-market risks.
  • Positioning within credit across investment-grade (IG) credit, high-yield credit, and emerging markets (EM) all contributed to returns. Credit spreads tightened across the rating spectrum and sectors for most corporate bonds. Within IG credit, the energy sector was the primary contributor to returns. Our exposure to utilities issuers was also favorable. Within high-yield credit, communications exposure was one of the primary drivers of returns. Emerging-markets positioning was also additive, led by government-related issuers.

Positioning & Outlook

  • The Fund remains selective and positioned with low levels of risk relative to history in US high yield and EM high yield, where valuations appear compressed when accounting for forward-looking total-return potential.
  • We are keeping duration 2 higher relative to the portfolio's history to reflect better US Treasury prices and a higher probability of an easing Fed. The market has temporarily dismissed US fiscal-deficit worries. With high Treasury yields, historically tight credit spreads, and possible labor-market risks, we favor Treasuries. We believe the intermediate segment of the IG credit market represents the most attractive opportunity. Intermediate IG credit, currently our largest allocation, provides a combination of duration, credit quality, risk, and total return across diverse scenarios.
  • Macroeconomic conditions are uncertain, and ongoing geopolitical risks and fiscal deficits pose a headwind, but AI and inflation may provide a tailwind for fixed income. We're watching for AI-driven effects on growth, productivity, and the US labor market, but we anticipate this will be gradual. We believe growth could slow enough for Fed rate cuts without significantly harming corporate earnings in the short term. Over the medium term, risks increase for a sharper labor-market slowdown as tariffs, immigration policies, and AI impact the economy.

Hartford Dynamic Bond Fund (HDBAX)

As interest rates rise, bond prices generally fall. • The Fund may engage in active and frequent trading to achieve its objective. As a result, the Fund is expected to have high portfolio turnover, which will increase its transaction costs and could increase an investor's tax liability. • Investments in high-yield ("junk") bonds involve greater risk of price volatility, illiquidity, and default than higher-rated debt securities. • Foreign investments, including foreign government debt, may be more volatile and less liquid than U.S. investments and are subject to the risk of currency fluctuations and adverse political, economic and regulatory developments. These risks may be greater, and include additional risks, for investments in emerging markets. • Derivatives are generally more volatile and sensitive to changes in market or economic conditions than other securities; their risks include currency, leverage, liquidity, index, pricing, valuation, and counterparty risk. • Restricted securities may be more difficult to sell and price than other securities. • Obligations of U.S. Government agencies are supported by varying degrees of credit but are generally not backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. Government.

data.title: Epsilon Energy: Newly Acquired Powder River Basin Assets Meaningfully Increases Leverage
data.description:
Intro

Epsilon Energy Ltd. (EPSN) is an onshore oil and gas company operating in Oklahoma, Pennsylvania, New Mexico, and Texas. We wrote about it back in March of this year, when we decided to take a cautious stance as opposed to our bullish recommendation in the previous year. Our downgrade to a 'Hold' mainly stemmed from the company's overhanging valuation (specifically the price of Epsilon's earnings) at the time. The downgrade ended up being the right call, as shares have lost approximately 34% of their value over the past eight months. In fact, as we consult EPSN's intermediate chart, we see that the breakout above the 2022 highs only lasted temporarily before turning over (the Stock topped out in June) into a sustained pattern of lower highs and lower lows. Epsilon stock has come up against some downside support in recent sessions, which has stabilized matters somewhat for the time being. Considering Epsilon's mere $127 million market cap, however, elevated near-term risk remains that we may see lower prices, which recent trends seem to be pointing to.

Due to the magnitude of the downswing over the past five months, Epsilon stock has become heavily oversold, as confirmed by the ultra-sold nature of the intermediate MACD. However, the death-cross (crossing of the 10-week moving average beneath its 40-week counterpart), which took place earlier this, trumps any other technical indicator as it establishes the trend in motion. Therefore, we may indeed have more downside here, especially if the $4.25 to $4.40 fails to provide support over the near term.

The strength of the downtrend in recent months is also a factor, as demonstrated by the stock's rising ADX trend-following indicator. Since established trends tend to persist rather than reverse, the probability is that lower lows or consolidation, at best, is on the cards for Epsilon over the near term.

Remember, the technical charts always instantly let us know how the market (investors) views the company in question. Therefore, whilst Epsilon continues to have serious potential from a leveraging standpoint with respect to the assets it has on its books, searing earnings growth (which would consequently spike the share price) isn't on investors' radar at present, all things remaining equal.

Q3 Earnings

Epsilon reported its third-quarter earnings for the present fiscal year, where GAAP earnings of $0.05 per share came in on reported revenues of $8.98 million (23% year-over-year gain). The CEO outlined the success of the Permian asset to date, noting that drilling and completion of the eighth well proceeded in the quarter. Epsilon's Permian assets continue to give the company oxygen with respect to its cash flow, noting that 40%+ of operating cash flow has already been reaped from the company's spend to date in this asset.

Furthermore, it shouldn't be understated how Epsilon's Q3 revenues grew by over 23%, considering curtailments (concerning Nat-Gas related production) in the Marcellus continue to abound. These types of trends speak to the strength of Epsilon's balance sheet in that (in a commodity-driven environment), it can carry a significant amount of non-producing assets on its books, yet has the capacity to continue to invest in the company aggressively, growing top-line revenues as a result.

Peak Companies Acquisition

What overshadowed the earnings release was updates on the Peak Companies acquisition, which was outlined in August of this year, relating to operated assets in the Powder River Basin. Official releases concerning the closing of this deal (with respect to the issuance of shares, etc) came out later in the month, and it's good to see the share price holding its own, post the closing of the acquisition. In fact, the CFO pointed out that if one were to study the assets which have come onto Epsilon's books and correlated this with Epsilon's share-price action since negotiations commenced, shares are actually cheaper now (from an asset standpoint), despite lower lows in recent months.

One thing to mention on the acquisition, the stock price movement since we first negotiated the deal has worked in our favour from a valuation perspective on the acquired assets. The deal is for a set number of shares to be issued at closing, plus the assumption of debt. Using, for example, $5 per share for Epsilon common, we are acquiring core undeveloped net acreage in the PRB at less than $900 per acre or, thought of another way, paying less than $300,000 per priority location. Both of those metrics, we believe to be discounts to market value.

Significant Operating Leverage Now At Epsilon's Disposal

Notwithstanding the optimization work Epsilon can do on producing wells in the Powder River Basin, the undeveloped inventory in this deal is vast, which is excellent from a financial standpoint, concerning cost reductions that can accrue. Furthermore, as mentioned, with the price of Natural Gas at levels aligned to 2009, we believe it is only a matter of time before this commodity breaks out of its multi-year-long trading range. Once curtailments lift in the Marcellus, we expect to see significant cash-flow gains over time, with the Auburn area having been earmarked for development. Furthermore, the COO laid out how continued Permian strength is expected in 2026, with ongoing discussions regarding an operation for Epsilon's Canadian JV.

Our Permian Basin Barnett project continues to be a solid performer. The eighth well in the play is performing very consistently compared with the first 7 wells. We now have 2 net wells making approximately 575 barrels of oil equivalent per day in the project. At least 2 more Barnett wells, 0.5 net, are planned for 2026. In our Canadian JV, we are in discussions with the operator on potential plans for the next 18 months. And lastly, the company is in the early stages of exploring a sale of our non-core Mid-Con assets in Oklahoma.

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, although Epsilon's asset quantity & quality continue to improve, we must remember that the success of these assets is primarily commodity-driven, all things remaining equal. In saying this, management is confident that cash flows will continue to cover the dividend, which presently comes in at 5.4%. Dividend affordability and ongoing hedging limits provide downside protection, enabling investors to hold their positions until trading conditions become more favorable. Maintaining 'Hold' rating. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Gold Price Up 2.5% For The Week. Is A Break Of $4200/Oz A Certainty?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Gold prices are currently maintaining a robust uptrend and trading near two-week highs, around the $4150/oz handle. The precious metal is poised to log its fourth consecutive monthly gain, building on a record-setting surge experienced in October that briefly targeted the $4,400 area.

The continuation of the bullish rally could be a sign that the current rally still has deep support. The bullish trend is reinforced by technical indicators that confirm strong underlying momentum, following a selloff that allowed some profit taking.

Foundational Drivers: The Federal Reserve Pivot Proxy

The current rally and supporting gold's valuation is the aggressive market expectation of impending monetary policy easing by the US Federal Reserve. Current forecasts indicate an 84% probability of a US interest rate cut in December, with broader market consensus pricing in approximately 90 basis points of easing by the end of 2026.

Market participants are expecting the Fed to start cutting rates despite mixed rhetoric. The expectations do keep the US dollar (USD) under broad pressure and force US Treasury yields to drift lower. Gold is a beneficiary in this environment as declining real yields reduce the opportunity cost of holding the metal.

These easing expectations are validated by a sequence of disappointing US economic releases, including softer ADP employment numbers, weaker retail sales figures, and a notable drop in the Conference Board’s Consumer Confidence reading for November. Market participants are continuing a recent trend by actively front-running the central bank’s mandated reaction function.

Technical Outlook - Gold (XAU/USD)

Looking at the four-hour chart below, the technical picture is strong. Price action looks favorable with the RSI above 50, a sign of the bullish momentum.

Price continues to trade some way away from both the 50 and 100-day MA. This could lead to a short-term pullback at some stage.

Given the Thanksgiving holiday in the US tomorrow, markets could see lower levels of liquidity. This could keep gold prices range-bound as well.

Keep a close watch on Ukraine-Russia developments. Any sign that a deal may be edging close could lead to increased selling pressure on gold and thus push gold prices lower.

Gold (XAU/USD) Four-Hour Chart, November 26, 2025

Gold: Key Near-Term Technical Levels

As mentioned above, bulls may struggle to break beyond the $4200/ounce heading into the weekend as liquidity is expected to be thin. This is not to say it is not a possibility.

Key levels to pay attention to include

  • $4,380: All-Time High/Previous Record High (October 17)
  • $4,220: Critical Breakout Resistance Key threshold for acceleration
  • $4042: Key to Uptrend Integrity

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BLOK: High-Tech, High-Growth, Less Heartburn
data.description:
There are many alternatives when it comes to investing in the crypto ecosystem, looking beyond just bitcoins and bitcoin mining companies. I recently wrote about an AI-powered Schwab Crypto Thematic ETF (STCE), and found it a decent Buy. The Amplify Blockchain Technology ETF (BLOK) is another name I recommend. It has been an outperformer, remaining with the top half of the crypto pack (say STCE, DAPP, BKCH) in most market conditions and has some structural advantages (that we will discuss in this article) that make it a long term Buy.

The volatility of the crypto space is huge and investors need to have high growth high risk appetite to be able to ride this ETF. Also, to cut idiosyncratic risks, I particularly emphasize a basket of ETFs each having their own strengths. So, while STCE does find a place in that portfolio for its unbiased rules-based but active-like positioning (backed by performance data), BLOK's active strategy presents a decent balance of investments beyond mining and bitcoin prices, optional plays in AI and a well-diversified portfolio (low single-stock risks).

Methodology and Portfolio

BLOK's investment universe starts from companies meaningfully involved in blockchain development, usage, R&D or commercialization. Managers check for blockchain relevance (including profiting from blockchain based data). The portfolio of 40-60 stocks comprises a 70% core holding in companies who derive significant revenue from blockchain. The remaining portfolio (secondary ~30%) consists of companies having partnerships and investments in the space. Stocks are weighted equally within each group, although the active methodology means deviations over time (particularly given the portfolio size and idiosyncratic volatile moves between stocks). However, individual stock risk looks relatively muted compared to STCE (top weight ~11.3%), VanEck Digital Transformation ETF (DAPP) (top weight ~8.64%) and Global X Blockchain ETF (BKCH) (~13.12%). The top 10 holdings account for a reasonable ~35% of the total portfolio — not too concentrated. For comparison here are the top 10 total weightings: for DAPP ~60%, BKCH ~71% and STCE~60%. The diversification is a real structural strength for a crypto-themed ETF and should help protect the portfolio from adverse movements in single stocks.

Inferences from the Portfolio

Based on a manual scan of the current holdings, I found ~22% investments in bitcoin miners and infrastructure, names like HUT 8 Corp (HUT), Cipher Mining, Inc. (CIFR) and TeraWulf, Inc. (WULF). This is the most correlated part of BLOK to crypto prices and is reasonably low exposure to that extent. Adding another ~8% exposure in bitcoin ETFs and Trusts like iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) or even Strategy Inc (MSTR), the total bitcoin correlated part of the portfolio adds up to only ~30%. The bulk of the portfolio benefits beyond simply bitcoin price moves, unlike a mining focused ETF like say CoinShares Bitcoin Mining ETF (WGMI).

Exchanges and Brokerages account for a significant ~24% of the portfolio — companies like Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD), Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN) and CME Group, Inc. (CME). These companies generate revenues from trading and services fees. They need transaction volumes and not bitcoin price appreciation directly. They can benefit from greater bitcoin adoption and trading, so lower volatility over time in crypto prices is more important than a bull market alone (of course, a bull market helps).

Then there is a ~17% exposure in payments and fintech to companies like PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL), NU Holdings Ltd. (NU) and even MercadoLibre, Inc. (MELI). This layer is very diversified on its own with companies like MercadoLibre's share prices having very low dependency on bitcoin prices. Even the more dependent names like Paypal and Nu Holdings will benefit from increased crypto adoption (i.e. if bitcoin becomes a more accepted currency globally) versus sheer price appreciation.

BLOK also has ~15% in enterprise blockchain and AI-adjacent large caps — NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) and International Business Machines Corporation (IBM) are in this mix. They walk into the ETF on the basis of infrastructure support to mining and blockchain technology (from chips to cloud support). This is a very good diversification away from crypto prices and happens to have an AI optionality too in a lot of cases (like Nvidia and AMD).

Performance Insights

The diversification by theme and a breakaway from the crypto prices provide substantial alpha avenues for BLOK. BLOK could grow in many ways: AI compute, semis, fintech volumes, enterprise software spending or even tokenization. The bulk of BLOK doesn’t need Bitcoin prices to rise - it only needs Bitcoin to keep getting adopted. Adoption happens when volatility falls, not when prices spike alone.

Even within the ~30% of the portfolio more directly linked with bitcoin prices, surprises happen. Some traditional miners are turning into AI compute and hosting, like TeraWulf and Core Scientific, Inc. (CORZ). These shares actually led to a significant surge in BLOK at a time when bitcoin prices did not rise at all. At some point in October, BLOK was up 50% while bitcoin was still flat.

This AI side story was of course very well captured in a bitcoin mining focused ETF like WGMI where IREN Limited (IREN) and Cipher Mining (both together account for over a third of the overall portfolio). WGMI tripled in October due to this evolving story, but has also fallen by 42% since. WGMI's volatility is actually what makes BLOK's diversification more risk aware, both in terms of single stock exposure as well as exposure to several agnostic themes.

I have already charted the outperformance in BLOK versus IBIT (a proxy for bitcoin), illustrating its reward potential versus bitcoins. This comes at the cost of volatility that is lower than bitcoin's. In terms of standard deviations, BLOK shows 10% lower standard deviation than IBIT and less than half of WGMI. Both empirically, and in terms of how the portfolio is structured, expect BLOK to have a far superior risk-reward asymmetry than IBIT or WGMI like bitcoin focused bets.

A comparison with other crypto-themed ETFs also show BLOK has been in the top 2 most of the time over the past 3 years (observe the trajectory and not only the end point outperformance). Here are the standard deviation numbers for the risk/volatility perspective - BLOK ~45, STCE ~63, BKCH ~77 and DAPP ~74. Overall, BLOK is consistently towards the higher end of the total return spectrum within the crypto pack, while its risks are far lower. That calls for overweighing BLOK in any crypto and blockchain portfolio.

Overweight BLOK

I rate BLOK a Buy backed by several empirical evidences and structural advantages. First, its single stock diversification is best-in-class. Secondly, thematic diversification means lower volatility. Third, empirical performance shows these expectations actually translate to real life performance. BLOK outperforms most of the crypto pack most of the time and keeps volatility levels low.

Overall, I am bullish this ETF segment, that invests on a crypto ecosystem rather than just bitcoins and mining. This positions it to gain from simply better adoption over bitcoin appreciation. With regulatory supports coming in, in the US as well as elsewhere, as bitcoin adoption grows, this theme will likely keep growing. Crypto investing cannot be defensive given its volatility, so investors should have the risk appetite to wade through 50% MTM losses at some point. A systematic accumulation over time works. At least the wider crypto plays makes for lower volatility levels - but still may require similar levels of risk appetite. The overall crypto ecosystem bet looks bullish otherwise, with BLOK a material overweight in that portfolio.
